questions.question: While performing power-off stalls, a blockage in the induction system results in failure of your engine, at 5,500 feet. The elevation of the nearest airport due west, 6.5 NM away, is 826 feet. The average winds aloft from the surface to your altitude is 200° at 23 knots. If the POH dictates a calm-wind glide ratio of 11.57:1, and assuming each knot of headwind decreases your range by 0.2 NM, will you make the airport, and by how many miles?

choices.text: You will make the airport with .8 NM to spare
You will make the airport with 2.4 NM to spare

questions.correctChoice: You will make the airport with .8 NM to spare

questions.question: You recall that in the "Gimli Glider" incident, the Boeing 767 achieved a glide ratio of 12.15:1. If you were instead in the same 767 with the same conditions as the accident, how much further would you be able to stretch your glide?
questions.context: Referenced question 8: While performing power-off stalls, a blockage in the induction system results in failure of your engine, at 5,500 feet. The elevation of the nearest airport due west, 6.5 NM away, is 826 feet. The average winds aloft from the surface to your altitude is 200° at 23 knots. If the POH dictates a calm-wind glide ratio of 11.57:1, and assuming each knot of headwind decreases your range by 0.2 NM, will you make the airport, and by how many miles?

questions.question: Reference the previous question. With a glide ratio of 9:1 at what altitude would you have to be to glide to shore from the middle of the lake in calm wind? Lake elevation is 650'.
questions.context: Referenced question 17: You are flying to Oshkosh and debating flying over Lake Michigan, or going around. The narrowest portion is 56 NM along a 286° course cruising at 106 KTAS. If the winds are 325° at 15 kts how far after leaving the eastern shore would it be an equal amount of time to continue or turn around?

questions.question: In the last question, we assumed that the fuel leak rate had been constant since takeoff. Unfortunately, this was not the case. On the way to the operationally favorable diversion airport at 2300' AGL, your only engine has stopped producing power. Your fuel flow gauge shows zero GALS per hour. Your fuel quantity gauge shows zero on both sides. How did you not catch this sooner? There is an airport 5.4 NM away and the average glide angle to the airport will yield 400'/NM. Do you have sufficient energy to make it to that airport? Choose the answer that best describes your situation.

choices.text: No, you will be .3 NM short.
No, your altitude at the airport would have been -320' AGL (BGL?).
Yes, your altitude at the airport will be 140' AGL.
Yes, your altitude at the airport will be 340' AGL.

questions.correctChoice: Yes, your altitude at the airport will be 140' AGL.
questions.context: Referenced question 16: Imagine that the fuel quantity gauges are showing a total of 50 GALS on board, and the "total fuel used" gauge is showing 27.2 GALS used over the past 2 HRS and 17 MINS. At departure, you had 86 GALS on board. You have a fuel leak between the tank and the fuel flow sensor that is causing the difference, and you want to know if you'll have enough fuel to get to the more operationally favorable diversion airport, rather than the closest in point of time. To get to the operationally favorable diversion airport you will burn 13 GALS cruise, descent, and landing, and those steps will require 48 MINS. You'd like to land with at least 6 GALS on board as a safe reserve. If you assume that the leak rate has been constant since takeoff, and divert now, how much fuel will you have on landing at the operationally favorable diversion airport?

questions.question: A German sailplane with a 28 meter wingspan has a glide ratio of 65:1. How far will it glide in a straight line from 7,500' AGL to touchdown under calm winds?

The POH for your single engine aircraft specifies a 12:1 glide ratio if the engine were to fail. If you were cruising at 8,000 feet over the Gulf of Mexico, how far away from the shoreline could you be if you wanted to remain within gliding range of land?

Your engine has been acting funny during climb, but it is a beautiful day so you decide to go fly anyway. After takeoff you climb at 830 fpm for a mere 1.7 minutes before your engine fails. With a 14:1 glide ratio, how far will you travel before crash landing.

One afternoon you are preparing for a lesson in a Cessna 172, and your instructor provides you with this hypothetical situation: At 7500 ft MSL, you lose your engine. The winds aloft are 120° at 25 knots all the way to the surface. Airport #1 (field elevation of 3100 ft MSL) is 10 NM on a true course of 310°. Airport #2 (field elevation of 350 ft MSL) is 13 NM on a true course of 145°. Your glide rate of descent is 700 FPM and your average TAS is 80 knots. Assuming instantaneous turns to the airports, which, if any, airport can you make it to?

choices.text: You can make both but only barely by 0.1 to 0.25 nautical miles.
You come up short to both airports by more than 1 nautical mile.
You come up short to Airport #1 by 1.2 nautical miles but can land at Airport #2.
You can land at Airport #1 but come up short to Airport #2 by 3.2 nautical miles.

questions.correctChoice: You can land at Airport #1 but come up short to Airport #2 by 3.2 nautical miles.
